item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any subject still aboard SCP-1260 at this time will become irretrievable.

All this was intended to convey was that once the transmission cease, anyone aboard SCP-1260 is unable to get off at that point. However, that is more than implied in the previous sentences in the paragraph, and so I've removed that line, as well as the bit in the description regarding them just leaving the remains aboard. I've also clarified that the recovered remains of Exploration Team 1 were, in fact, removed from the ship.

There isn't some big mystery with this regard, I'm afraid. Just some lack of clarity on my part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So there is this part:

Any subject still aboard SCP-1260 at this time will become irretrievable.

which is never explained, which wouldn't be so bad if not for this:

All bodies have now been recovered

Maybe I didn't understand it fully, but how it reads to me is: the second exploration team found all the bodies of the first team and reported them as "recovered", which means (to me) that they are basically taking them back to SCPS Something. In the italicised text below, there is no mention of the bodies but also no mention of the difficulties encountered when trying to recover them. And the description clearly mentions that the first team still lies aboard the ghost ship. So what really happened? They never tried to take their dead collegues back? Or they tried and were stopped somehow? And why they didn't/couldn't try?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your critique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a ghost ship. That's it. Fair enough but it just didn't really interest me. I wish I had been around to see the carvings as I think they may have fleshed out the origins of the ship (pardon the pun). I also am not a huge fan of the final entry of the team leader. I would have thought that the Foundation picks folks made of sterner stuff to lead first exploration teams. Using your last words to say "Fuck you!" after knowingly boarding an anomalous ship and logically knowing the risks associated seems trite a best and childish at worst. No vote for me.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Confirming removal of the above image, as the author of this SCP article.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) as, according to this source, the image comes from tallshipbounty.org whose terms of service only allow for non-commercial use, AKA "NC". Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Sure thing, I'll keep the timer up then.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I dig it. This has my approval for implementation, though I'd like for the timer to run down so that anyone else with other ideas has a chance to voice them.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer removed and edits made per author request.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block 
- hide block

After speaking with Conwell about this article, a link to SCP-2467 was mentioned after discussion took place on what the implications of 1260 and 2467 coming into contact with one another would be. SCP-2467, as we know, actively searches for other vessels and and takes their crew and cargo, and in the instance of SCP-1260, once contact with it is made, the ship will disappear and reappear elsewhere. There's a lot of possible things that could happen if the two ever were to come into contact with one another, so I'd like to propose the addition of the following line in the containment procedures:

Editing this:

Any vessel attempting to navigate the waters within a 20-km radius of SCP-1260 is to be diverted by Foundation personnel aboard the SCPS Cassandra.

To read:

Any vessel attempting to navigate the waters within a 20-km radius of SCP-1260 is to be diverted by Foundation personnel aboard the SCPS Cassandra. Should SCP-2467 come within 50 km of SCP-1260, containment staff are to immediately begin emergency relocation procedures to prevent SCP-1260's assimilation.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this and the part at the end where you where kind of left without answers (In a good way that shows we can´t understand Everything) just made me "happy" if you could call it that. Ok, to put it simple, It was good.

But the Agents log felt, abit un-terrefying. He didn´t feel too scared.
I will rewrite it to make it creepier and I will repost it here for you to decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you liked the article.

To answer why I didn't include the carvings in a log, let me first say that in the original version of this article (waaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaay back when it was a wee draft) I had a rather large log of them. I took that log out during the review phase, for as many people were quick to point out:

"There is only so many ways you can express sheer terror through graffiti"

In the end they were axed. Trust me when I say that the reader's ideas for what the carvings say is probably more interesting than what I would have included.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what is it with the Foundation and ships (specially Ghostships) but it usually works. Same here, this scip is intriguing, eerie and downright creepy at times, it leaves a lot of questions unanswered, but maybe that's the point: we don't freaking know a thing about this ship and what it does, or why, or where does it go to… It's not not so much as "lacking" but "intentionally omitting information" and I think that's amazingly done. One thing, though, I would love to read some of the carvings. You just mention their existence and not much else (the ones left by the exploration team don't count).

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree. I was on the fence a while but I think it's a fine take on an old cliche.
But I also am a sucker for everything extradimensional. I was inclined to not vote until I saw the other world.
Also congrats on +75. Not easy to write this one to Foundation standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting idea, good execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one. Looking back at old comments, seems there were past problems, but as it stands now, I think it's brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'd like it better without the end note by the Agent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It needs a reason, or at least a hint of a reason. Maybe have some clues in the grafiti?

For my money, it makes no sense that the captain didn't make an entry about the dimension of darkness his ship fell into. I mean, the agent had plenty of time to write a thorough and coherent entry, and captains of that era were known to make entries while their ships were on fire or sinking in hurricanes. But it also makes it creepy in a "Mary Celeste" sort of way to have the log-book as if nothing at all happened, so i can see how others might disagree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but we already have something almost exactly like it. I'll see if I can find the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We've… Never done a Twilight Zone thing… Have we?

And… SCP idea found. Though it kind of sucks. Will run it by people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't grab me at all. It's just a ghost ship, like the most basic sort of ghost ship you could possibly have. Also, your picture doesn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You cross his venerable lordship with everything…
It's hard to resist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this and am tempted to have a certain Lordship cross paths with it at some point in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the fact that it's a rather boring thing-with-fog-that-leads-to-another-dimension, there's some minor tonal issues. "Clutching" is something you would never say in real life, "holding" is more clinical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well written, easy to understand and follow, but I felt there was something missing, some sort of twist or something, I'm not sure.

I'm sorry I can't be more specific, but I just feel something slightly more could be done with it. I'll add more here if I can put my finger on why it sits oddly in my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damn it now I want to make this into a tale…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your in a world of black seas and blacker skies, where the only life is a whispering in the distance. Welcome to the HMS Tiresias, where the crew are all skeletons for some reason. This unwitting team has sailed through…

The Twilight Zone.

That's why you should avoid making this an audio log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll be sure to remove the piece on the "informal" writing. Looking over it again, I think you were being gracious with the mack truck analogy.

I'm I think I'll make the Agent's note into an audio recording after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's rare that I get to say this about informal portions of an article, but the note left in the back of the Captain's log has some really weird tone issues- by which I mean, it doesn't sound right for a dying team leader putting his last words on paper. It's a little purple, it doesn't come across as sincere, and that opening sentence about "informal" writing hits the fourth wall like a mack truck. We get some exposition out of it, and little else.

The rest of the article is mostly unremarkable. Not bad, just not terribly compelling. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Definitely something I will consider adding to the Agent's Log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe something when they sail towards the whispers, or something when they anchor Abd DON'T go to them. Just ideas, like I said, it stands on its own as is. But it could expand more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, any suggestions? What would you like to see elaborated?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not bad. It just feels… Lacking. Concept is there, it even WORKS. Execution is good, but I feel like you could have elaborated more. You are fine with it now, but I'd like to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see. Thank you for the honesty.
I'll wait for bit more feedback before I make such an edit then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since I generally avoid audio logs like the plague, it wouldn't help me in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would changing the note in the Captain's log to an audio recording perhaps improve the article? This would show the Agent's emotions better and allow for me to include reference to the voices via background recordings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. This article can go further. As it is now it is a bit boring, in a wat that leaves you feeling that this article could be lovely if it went more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh shit. I thought I addressed that in a previous edit.
Quickly, to the editing page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This entry as a whole isn't very interesting. I would have liked to see more flavorful text in the logs. As it is currently, it feels like a weak version of the cargo ship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really do like this. Some bits of the exploration logs and the Agent's Note sound just a tad too formal for normal speech, but other than that, it's a great awesomely-written unique ghost ship thingy.

One plot hole though: even if the realm with the black seas is toxic/fatal/makes people rot away with magicks instead of starvation, why did that stop all 200-something people from eating the food? They said the supplies were untouched.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you everyone who helped me with this when it was just a draft.

JC
